raw_case:
Identifying the unlikely suspect: A massive patellar metastasis from esophageal squamous cell carcinoma—A case report
Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma ranks as the eighth most common global cancer, known for its aggressive metastatic potential. This report presents a rare case of a 67-year-old male with a history of cervical esophagus SCC, who developed an unusual metastasis to the patella, a site exceedingly rare for metastasis. The patient, with a history of localized spread and various treatments, presented with leukocytosis, anemia, and progressively worsening right knee pain. MRI findings revealed a significant mass at the patella's upper pole, indicative of metastatic involvement, leading to successful surgical intervention at a specialized facility. This case underscores the importance of considering atypical metastatic sites in patients with known ESCC, highlighting the complexity of managing such rare metastatic presentations and the necessity for a tailored, multidisciplinary approach in diagnosis and treatment.

Introduction

Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC), characterized by its aggressive progression and significant mortality, is currently the eighth most common cancer worldwide and sixth leading cause of cancer-related death [1]. The management of esophageal cancer is multifaceted, often requiring a combination of surgical, chemotherapeutic, and radiotherapeutic strategies. Despite the curative intent of treatment, nearly half of the patients develop metastases within 5 years [2]. Common sites of metastasis include the lungs, liver, and bones—organs with rich vascular networks that are prone to infiltration by circulating tumor cells [3]. Although bone metastases are relatively common in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), their occurrence in the patella is exceedingly rare, with only 3 known cases reported thus far [[4], [5], [6]]. Moreover, even in cases of ESCC spreading into unexpected areas of the body, only 8% of them originate from the upper esophagus [7]. This case report details a 67-year-old meal with the first known instance of upper esophageal squamous cell carcinoma metastasizing to the patella.

Case report

A 67-year-old male was referred by an oncologist for evaluation due to the presence of leukocytosis and anemia. The patient's initial complaints included progressively intensifying pain in the right knee, described as both stabbing and constricting in nature, which he had been experiencing for approximately 2 months. The physical examination revealed limited movement in the patellar region, accompanied by noticeable discomfort during range-of-motion assessments. No signs of erythema or rubor were present around the joint with palpable distal pulses.The patient's medical history was significant for an extensive oncological profile. He was diagnosed 7 years prior, in 2015, with locally advanced cervical esophagus SCC and was treated with concurrent chemotherapy and radiotherapy, specifically carboplatin and paclitaxel. Subsequent oncological events included isolated metastases to the lung detected in 2016 and in 2020. In 2021, a substantial paraspinal and chest wall mass measuring up to 10 cm was identified and biopsied, confirming the continuation of SCC. The initial therapeutic strategy comprised capecitabine and cisplatin, which was later adjusted to nivolumab following the onset of hypothyroidism and indications of advancing disease.At the time of the current admission, the patient presented with no signs of additional metastatic spread to distal parts of the body, including the liver and head. With an initial suspicion of an infectious etiology, the patient underwent a comprehensive workup and received empirical antibiotic treatment. However, all investigations yielded negative results, excluding infection as a cause.An MRI of the knee was subsequently performed, revealing a mass with aggressive characteristics originating from the upper pole of the patella. The mass, displaying lobulated borders and encompassing the quadriceps tendon, measured approximately 4.6 × 2.9 × 4.2 cm. Details of the MRI fin dings are given in Fig. 1.These radiologic findings raised significant concerns for a metastatic lesion. A biopsy was talken which confirmed the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) as shown in the Fig. 2. After discussing the case with the in-house orthopedics team, he was transferred to continue care under an orthopedic oncologist. The patient promptly underwent surgical intervention, which was successfully executed at the referred facility.

Discussion

Primary patellar tumors are extremely rare, accounting for <1% of primary bone tumors, yet they are more common than metastatic deposits and are usually benign with the most common one being giant cell tumors (8). Patellar metastasis from esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) is an extraordinarily rare event, with only a handful of cases reported in medical literature. It commonly present as a recurrent knee pain is a common complaint which can be easily overlooked, especially in elderly population.Common metastatic sites of esophageal cancer are to regional lymph nodes or nearby organs like liver and lung [8,9]. Metastatic spread to the patella is uncommon due to the relatively low vascularity of the region compared to other bones such as the spine, pelvis, and long bones. The mechanism of metastasis in this case likely involves hematogenous dissemination, where circulating tumor cells adhere to the endothelium of capillaries in distant organs, eventually invading and proliferating within the bone tissue. Studies suggest that the bone microenvironment, rich in growth factors and cytokines, plays a crucial role in facilitating tumor cell colonization and growth [10].MRI remains the gold standard for detecting bone metastases due to its high sensitivity and ability to delineate soft tissue structures. The aggressive nature of the lesion, as evidenced by the lobulated borders and involvement of the quadriceps tendon, was indicative of a metastatic process rather than a primary bone tumor [11]. The management of patellar metastasis involves a multidisciplinary approach. The role of systemic therapy, including chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy, is crucial in controlling the primary disease and preventing further metastatic spread.The use of nivolumab in this patient points to the evolving field of immunotherapy in treating advanced ESCC, offering potential for prolonged survival even in cases with metastatic disease [12]. Surgical management of metastatic spread to the patella typically involves resection of the tumor to alleviate pain and restore joint function. This is often followed by reconstruction or stabilization procedures to ensure structural integrity and facilitate rehabilitation, aiming to improve the patient's quality of life and mobility [13].This case report illustrates the rare phenomenon of patellar metastasis from esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, emphasizing the necessity for a high index of suspicion and thorough diagnostic evaluation in patients with a history of cancer presenting with unusual musculoskeletal symptoms especially in elderly patients. Further studies are warranted to enhance our understanding and improve the clinical outcomes for patients with this rare manifestation of metastatic disease.

Conclusion

This case report highlights an extraordinary instance of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) metastasizing to the patella in a 67-year-old male, a site notably rare for metastasis. The patient, initially presenting with progressively worsening right knee pain, leukocytosis, and anemia, had a significant oncological history, including cervical esophagus SCC treated with chemotherapy and radiotherapy. MRI revealed a substantial metastatic mass at the patella's upper pole, necessitating successful surgical intervention. This case highlights the critical need for considering atypical metastatic sites in ESCC patients, advocating for a thorough, multidisciplinary approach to diagnosis and treatment to manage such rare metastatic presentations effectively.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 67-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressively worsening right knee pain described as stabbing and constricting.
- **History of Present Illness:** Two-month history of right knee pain with limitations in patellar movement and discomfort during range-of-motion assessments.
- **Past Medical History:** Diagnosed in 2015 with locally advanced cervical esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (SCC); treated with chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Subsequent isolated metastases to the lungs (2016, 2020) and a paraspinal and chest wall mass (2021). Long history of systemic therapy including capecitabine, cisplatin, and nivolumab.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Limited movement in the patellar region with discomfort during range-of-motion assessments. No erythema, rubor, or circulatory deficits.
- **Ancillary Tests:** MRI of the knee revealed an aggressive lobulated mass at the upper pole of the patella (4.6 × 2.9 × 4.2 cm). Biopsy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma. Investigations ruled out infection. No other distal metastatic sites detected.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - Infectious processes (e.g., septic arthritis): Suggested by knee pain, leukocytosis, and anemia but excluded after negative infection workup and absence of systemic infection signs. 
   - Inflammatory or degenerative joint diseases (e.g., osteoarthritis or gout): Unlikely due to the aggressive and localized nature of the patellar lesion on MRI.
   - Primary bone tumor (e.g., giant cell tumor): Possible as it is more common than metastasis in the patella. Considered but excluded after biopsy confirmed SCC.
   - Metastatic disease: Suspected due to the patient’s known history of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, aggressive MRI findings involving the quadriceps tendon, and the systemic nature of metastatic SCC.

2. **Confirmatory Steps:**
   - MRI findings of a lobulated, aggressive mass were suggestive of metastatic involvement.
   - Biopsy confirmed the lesion as squamous cell carcinoma, affirming metastasis from the patient’s known primary esophageal SCC.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Metastatic patellar lesion secondary to esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC), a highly rare and atypical metastasis site. Diagnosis was supported by imaging findings, biopsy results, and the patient’s oncological history. Infection and primary bone tumor were excluded.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Metastatic patellar lesion secondary to esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC).

checked_rare_disease: Squamous cell carcinoma of the esophagus

Reasoned for 6 seconds

### Reasoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 67-year-old male with a history of cervical esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) diagnosed in 2015, with subsequent metastatic involvement of the lungs and a paraspinal mass. This raises the possibility of metastasis being a central issue for the current presentation.

<step 2> The patient's chief complaint of progressively worsening right knee pain over the last two months, described as stabbing and constricting, combined with limited patellar movement and discomfort during range-of-motion assessments, suggests a musculoskeletal or localized pathology affecting the knee joint.

<step 3> Physical examination findings indicate discomfort during range-of-motion assessments but no signs of acute inflammation (no erythema or rubor), which helps rule out an infectious etiology or acute inflammatory joint disease.

<step 4> The MRI findings reveal an aggressive lobulated mass at the upper pole of the patella, measuring 4.6 × 2.9 × 4.2 cm. This finding, combined with the biopsy confirmation of squamous cell carcinoma, strongly suggests that the mass is metastatic in origin, most likely originating from the patient's previously diagnosed esophageal SCC.

<step 5> The absence of distal metastatic sites on further investigation helps focus the diagnosis on a localized metastasis rather than widespread disease, meaning the knee mass is most likely a solitary metastatic deposit from the patient’s prior SCC.

### Answer:
Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in the right knee.
